explanation: Mr Ignatius does not want his children to grow up in Nigeria. He said: "I want them to grow up in a sane environment. I don't want other children that are being trained outside the country now to return and lord things over my own children." This can be found on page 20 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What skill did Mrs Ignatius begin learning to support her family abroad?
explanation: Mrs Ignatius, in preparation to relocate abroad learnt tailoring and hairdressing. This can be found on page 20 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What disrupted the Ignatius family's relocation plans?
options: Mrs Ignatius changed her mind about relocating.
A DNA test revealed Mr Ignatius was not Favour's biological father.
explanation: During the visa processing, a DNA test reveals that Mr Ignatius is not the father of Favour, one of the children. As a result, the family never remained the same again. This can be found on page 20 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why was Mr Ayesoro transferred from his teaching role?
options: A student had recurring nightmares about him.
He requested a transfer for personal reasons.
The school wanted him to work in their property division.
explanation: One of Mr Ayesoro's students, Bibi had recurring nightmares about him because of his scary tribal marks. When Bibi;s mother laid a complaint at the school, the management transferred Mr Ayesoro to Stardom Hub, the property wing of Stardom Group of Companies. This can be found on page 22 and 23 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What nickname did students give to Mr Ayesoro because of his tribal marks?
explanation: Mr Ayesore was nicknamed Mr Owala because of his scary tribal mark. This can be found on page 22 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why did the school transfer Mr Ayesoro to Stardom Hub?
options: To provide him with a better job opportunity.
To reward him for his dedication.
To retain Mrs Ladele’s children in the school.
explanation: In The Lekki Headmaster, Mr. Ayesoro was transferred to Stardom Hub as part of the school's effort to keep Mrs. Ladele’s children enrolled. The school administration made this decision because Mrs. Ladele, a wealthy and influential parent, had made it clear that she would withdraw her children if Mr. Ayesoro was not transferred to the new branch. This can be found on page 23 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What does the MD discover about the land Stardom acquired two years ago?
options: It is being used for illegal activities.
It has been sold without her knowledge.
Teachers and staff are using it as a car park.
It is being developed into a new school building.
explanation: While Mrs Ibbidunn Gloss was taking a walk on the school property, she chose to check a piece of land that was acquired by the school two years ago. This is when she discovered that the staff at Stardom were using that piece of land as a car park even after the staff had been assigned parks within the premises. This can be found on page 24 and 25 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: How are the teachers and staff at Stardom able to afford cars?
options: They have taken loans from the school’s cooperative society.
explanation: The staff at Stardom Schools are able to afford cars because they take loans from the school's cooperative society. This can be found on page 25 and 26 of the novel where the principal reveals this to the MD after she found the cars parked in one of the school's properties that they have turned into a car park.

subject: English Language
q: How much money is in the account of the Stardom Cooperative Society, as discovered by the board?
explanation: The amount of money in the cooperative's purse is N95 million, while over N50 million had been loaned out. This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What simile does the MD use to describe the potential risk of the cooperative funds?
options: It’s like building castles in the air.
It’s like hanging a snake in the roof and going to bed.
It’s like lighting a fire in the middle of a haystack.
It’s like giving a sword to your enemy.
explanation: This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What decision does the board make about loans from the cooperative?
options: No loans can be given to staff under any circumstances.
All loan requests must be approved by the staff union.
The cooperative must stop lending money to staff immediately.
explanation: The board resolved that no staff member could borrow more than 250,000. This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why do some teachers at Stardom Schools dread Open Day?
options: They are afraid of being sacked
The day involves long hours of work
explanation: This can be found on page 27 of the novel where Openn Day is said to be dreaded by teachers because parents often came with "trailer-loads of complaints."

subject: English Language
q: What did Mr Guta demand in the MD’s office regarding Mr Fafore?
options: A salary increase for Mr Fafore
The immediate dismissal of Mr Fafore
A change in his son’s class teacher.
A public apology from Mr Fafore.
explanation: Mr Guta asked the MD to immediately sack Mr Fafore for the supposed "grammatical error" that Mr Fafore had taught his child. This can be found on page 27 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why does the school no longer allow students to copy notes from the Class Prefect?
options: A parent sued the school for this practice
The students often make errors when copying notes
The teachers believe it wastes time
explanation: The school no longer permitted students to copy notes from the class prefect because a parent once sued the school for this practice. The parent, an accountant with an oil company did not like how his daughter was exploited by the teachers as she was told to copy the notes on the board because she had good handwriting. This can be found on page 29 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What reason did Mr Guta give for being angry with Mr Fafore?
options: He found a grammatical error in his son's note
He felt Mr Fafore was not teaching effectively
He was unhappy with Mr Fafore's attitude
His son complained about being treated unfairly
explanation: Mr Guta was angry with Mr Fafore and wanted to get him fired simply because he spotted a supposed "grammatical error" in his son's note. It is eventually discovered that what he found was not a grammatical error but a properly constructed expression. This entire incident can be found in chapter six of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What grammatical rule was the source of disagreement between the MD and the principal?
options: The use of the subjunctive mood
The use of conjunctions such as (and)
The subject-verb agreement with phrases like (as well as)
explanation: The grammatical rule that was the source of disagreement is the "subject-verb agreement rule" with the expression "Ade as well as Jide comes vs. come." Mr Guta and the MD believed that Mr Fafore had taught the children the wrong thing when he instructed his students to write "Ade as well as Jide comes" in their notes. This was the reason Mr Fafore was sacked. However, the principal reveals to the MD later on that this expression is indeed accurate because when the construction "as well as" is used to join two noun subjects together, the singular verb (comes) is used. This can be found in chapter six of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What did Mr Audu do after the MD felt deflated that she had wrongly sacked Mr Fafore?
options: He defended Mr Fafore's teaching methods
He proposed new rules for grammar checks
He suggested a training session for the staff
He cracked a joke to lighten the mood
explanation: Mr Audu, the school's fine art teacher, and "clown" of the novel, in is usual fashion, cracked a joke to lighten the tense atmosphere brought on by the sad realisation that Mr Fafore had been wrongly sacked. He says "I just want to say that Mr Fafore,... as well as the principal ... is correct. And the MD, Mrs Ibidun Gloss, is hereby pardoned, discharged and acquitted." (The Lekki Headmaster: pg. 33).

subject: English Language
q: What conclusion did the teachers draw from Mr Fafore's sack incident?
options: Open Day is a stressful event
There is no job security in the establishment
Parents should not interfere with teaching methods
The MD is always fair in her decisions
explanation: Mr Fafore's sack incident reminded the teachers at Stardom School of the essential fact that there is no job security in the establishment. This was after Mr Fafore had come into the staff room to pack up his personal items. This can be found on page 31 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why did Bepo leave Beesway Group of School?
options: He was accused of sharing confidential information with the parents
He was dissatisfied with the grammatical error in the the school's name
He witnessed an event that he considered ungodly
He disagreed with the director about his salary
explanation: Bepo left Beesway Group of School after witnessing the ritual that occurred in the school's compound. He considered this practice ungodly and chose to leave the school. This can be found in chapter 7 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What grammatical issue did Bepo raise about Beesway Group of School's name?
options: The use of singular instead of plural in (Group of School)
The absence of a hyphen in the name
The misuse of capital letters in the name
explanation: The grammatical issue that Bepo had a problem with in the school's name was the use of singular in "Group of School". He tries to get the director to understand that when a collective noun like "group" is used, it should be followed by a plural noun as in "Group of Schools". This can be found on page 34 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why did the director of "Beesway Group of School" claim the ritual involving the cow was necessary?
options: To pray for his late father who gave him the land
To bring in more pupils to the school
To appease the parents who had raised complaints
To conduct a purification ceremony for the school
explanation: The director claimed the ritual was necessary because it was actually part of a special prayer for his late father, who had given him the land on which the school was built. This can be found on page 41 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What led to the closure of the school Bepo started?
options: Poor road conditions in the neighbourhood
A failed spiritual ritual to attract more pupils
explanation: Bepo's own school, "Fruitful Future School" was shut down because a lot of the parents moved out of the neighbourhood because the condition of the roads was worsening and the government did nothing about it. The parents that were left in the neighbourhood could not afford the fees and so Bepo had to shut the school down. This can be found on page 39 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What was the cost of the form for the position of Head Boy or Head Girl in the school election at Stardom?
explanation: The cost of the form for the position of Head Boy or Head Girl is N50,000. This can be found on page 41 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What inappropriate remark did Banky make during Speech Day?
options: Tosh was unqualified for the position
Tosh was the son of an ex-convict
Tosh bribed his way into the election
Tosh lacked the required skill for the role
explanation: During Speech Day, Banky said: "Instead of voting for the son of an ex-convict, cast your vote for me...". This can be found on page 42 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why was Tosh's father, Chief Ogba, detained for 36 months?
options: He failed to pay a school debt
He was accused of fraud but acquitted later
He faced trial for alleged misappropriation
explanation: Tosh's father, Chief Didi Ogba spent 36 months in detention because he was facing trial for alleged misappropriation of a 2.5 billion government contract sum. This can be found on page 42 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Which of the following waterfalls is the highest in West Africa, according to the novel?
explanation: According to the novel, Owu waterfalls in Kwara is the highest in West Africa, measuring 120 meters above water level and cascading 330 feet down an escarpment. This can be found on page 44 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What is the primary reason Mr Bepo organises excursions for the students at Stardom?
options: To give them a good grasp of their country before they all go abroad.
To promote Stardom as a school that values excursions
To prepare students for geography exams.
explanation: Bepo organised a lot of excursions for the students because he wanted them to have a "thorough grasp of their country." He knew that most of the students came from elite families that would eventually send them abroad after secondary education. This can be found on page 44 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
Which of the following festivals have the students attended? 

i. Calabar Carnival

ii. Osun Oshogbo Festival

iii. Argungu Festival

iv. Abuja Carnival
explanation: The students have attended the Calabar, Osun Osogbo and Argungu festivals. The Abuja festival was not included in the novel. This can be found on page 45 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: How did Mr Bepo inspire students after they visited areas like Mushin and Ajegunle during their excursions?
options: By promising to relocate them to better areas
By sharing stories of successful individuals who rose from slums
By discouraging them from visiting such areas again
By giving them motivational books about success
explanation: The students, during their excursion through places like Mushin, Ajegunle and Ijora, derided the area but Mr Bepo cautioned against that saying that being born in such areas does not determine one's success in life. He mentions successful people who once lived in slums but are now successful and live in places like Banana Island because they made use of their talents, money and opportunities. He mentioned people like Odion Ighalo and Victor Osimhen who grew up "in the low ends of Lagos." This can be found on page 46 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What was the source of the name "Badagry", according to the novel?
options: It was named after the Badagry River
It originated from a European explorer's name
It was named after a nearby slave market
explanation: The name Badagry originated from "Agbadarigi". This can be found on page 47 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What does Mr Bepo mean by the term "new slavery"?
options: Africans willingly move abroad for better opportunities
The continuation of slave trade in some regions
The exploitation of Africans by modern companies
Africans selling themselves into physical labour
explanation: The "new slavery" that Bepo refers to is "Africans themselves..., in Japa fit, voluntarily and desperately walking into the workforce of who seemed to be the yester masters." (The Lekki Headmaster: pg. 47)

subject: English Language
q: Why was Mr Bepo moved during his visit to the Black Heritage Museum?
options: He felt nostalgic about Nigeria's history
He was impressed by the museum's architecture
He empathised with the suffering of slaves in the past
He remembered his ancestors were involved in slavery
explanation: At the Black Heritage Museum, Bepo was emotionally moved by the memory of the ugly experiences the enslaved went through. This can be found on page 47 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What is the meaning of the term "japa" as used in the novel?
options: To travel abroad in search of better opportunities
To resist colonial influences inn modern times
To work as a slave in a foreign country
explanation: According to the novel, "to japa" means to relocate abroad in search of better opportunities. This idea is the central subject matter in the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why was Mr Bepo initially reluctant to renew his passport?
options: He was too busy with his teaching duties
He had heard about the hassles applicants were facing
He did not plan to travel abroad anytime soon
explanation: The passage states that Bepo had not been keen on relocating abroad, which is the main reason for his initial reluctance to renew his passport. Since he wasn't planning on traveling abroad, he didn't prioritize renewing his passport.

subject: English Language
q: How much was the official fee for a 10-year passport renewal (64 pages)?
explanation: This can be found on page 49 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why did Bepo choose Ibadan for his passport renewal?
options: It was closer to his location
He was advised that the process would be easier there
He had a friend who worked in immigration there
The fees were lower in Ibadan
explanation: Bepo knew that travelling to neighbouring states like Ogun, Oyo, Osun and Kwara would make the passport renewal easier and faster as there would be fewer crowds than in Lagos so he settled to renew his passport in Ibadan, Oyo State. This can be found on page 48 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What can be inferred about Tai's role in the passport renewal process?
options: He is a middleman exploiting applicants
He was a legitimate immigration officer
He was an honest businessman offering his services
He was unaware of the actual renewal process
explanation: According to the novel, Tai was no immigrant personnel. He was just an ordinary business centre operator working in cahoots with some immigration staff. While some would call the guy a consultant, others might brand him a recketeer. This can be found on page 52 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What does the phrase "in cahoots" most likely mean in the context of Tai and the immigration staff?
explanation: To be "in cahoots" with someone means to "secretly collaborate" with them, especially for the purpose of deceiving and exploiting others.

subject: English Language
q: Why does Mr Bepo delay his flight to the UK?
options: He is required to attend a farewell celebration
He needs to finalise his resignation paperwork
He has a last-minute meeting with the MD
explanation: Mr Bepo had to alter his departure date so that he could attend the farewell celebration planned for him by the school. The school agreed to pay the $100 fine that came with the alteration. This can e found on page 54 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What Yoruba adage does Mr Bepo recall after the debate at the farewell celebration?
options: A wise man never leaves his work unfinished
The work of a teacher outlives their time
Success is not final; failure is not fatal
Even if the master carver retires, his carvings remain
explanation: This can be found on page 55 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why does Mr Bepo yell "Noooo!" during the drama club performance at the farewell celebration?
options: He was disappointed with the dance
He is overwhelmed by memories of the Heritage Slave Museum
He feels the performance is disrespectful
He disagrees with the students' portrayal of history
explanation: While the drama class presented in front of Mr Bepo, they presented a Canoe dance of the Badagry people. This dance transported him back to Badagry where he had pictured the slaves and their agonies. This can be found on page 55 and 56 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What was the farewell gift presented to Mr Bepo?
options: A framed picture of the staff and students
A plaque commemorating his years of service
A plane ticket to his new destination
explanation: This can be found on page 58 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: At what time is Mr Bepo's flight to the UK scheduled to depart?
explanation: Bepo was billed to jet out with Emirates Airlines at 10:00pm on Saturday. This can be found on page 59 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Who offers to drive Mr Bepo to the airport?
explanation: Mr Ogunwale, Mr Bepo's landlord offered to drive him to the airport. This can be found on page 60 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What did Mr Bepo's wife insist he should pack for his trip to the UK?
options: Locust beans, egusi, ground crayfish and dry snail
explanation: His wife had advised Mr Bepo to pack iru (locust beans) and egusi (melon seeds) ground with crayfish and she also requested that he buy dry snails. This can be found on page 60 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What is the primary setting of The Lekki Headmaster​​​​​​?
explanation: The primary setting of the novel is Stardom Schools. This is where most of the events take place and it is also the source of Mr Bepo's conflict i.e. whether to keep working there as he has for more than two decades or to relocate abroad to join his wife and children.

subject: English Language
q: Who is the protagonist of the novel?
explanation: Mr Adewale Adebbepo aka Bepo aka The Lekki Headmaster is the protagonist of the novel. It is around him that the events in the novel are centred.

subject: English Language
q: What is Mr Bepo's profession?
explanation: Mr Bepo is the principal at Stardom Schools, Lekki.

subject: English Language
q: What is the main challenge that Mr Bepo faces in the novel?
options: Convincing parents to enrol their children
Balancing his career with pressure to migrate
explanation: The central challenge that Mr Bepo faces is the intense pressure to migrate to the UK even when he wants to stay at Stardom School to finish the good work he started. The novel begins with the dramatic incident at the morning assembly where he bursts into tears when he is reminded of the fact that he will be leaving for the UK soon. The story ends with his return to Stardom Schools after he made the decision not to go to the UK.

subject: English Language
q: What theme is predominantly explored in the novel?
explanation: The predominant theme in the novel is migration and identity. The author uses Mr Bepo's experiences as well as stories of migrants to other countries including the UK, Canada and USA to explore how the good and bad sides of relocating abroad and the resulting question of identity.

subject: English Language
q: Which teacher is known for the use of witty remarks in the novel?
explanation: Mr Audu, the Fina Arts teacher, is known for his well-timed witty remark. In chapter 1 of the novel, he refers to the MD as "a witch and a wizard rolled into one". He is also the one who coined the name "The Lekki Headmaster" for Mr Bepo.

subject: English Language
q: Which two students have a rivalry that dates back to JSS 3?
explanation: Banky and Tosh are SSS2 students who have a rivalry that dates back to JSS 3. Both students clashed in the Best Dancer category at the end-of-year party but Banky was crowned winner. This led Tosh's to threaten a lawsuit because they felt slighted. This can be found on page 42 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: The novel explores the effects of migration under which term?
explanation: The central theme in the novel is one of migration. The novel explores how people "japa" to other more developed countries in search of greener pastures.

subject: English Language
q: What led to the conflict during the prefect election speech?
options: Banky attempted to bribe the judges
Tosh's mom accused the school of favouritism
Bepo intervened in the voting process
explanation: The conflict arose during Speech Day when Banky said: "Instead of voting for the son of an ex-convict, cast your vote for me..." This can be found on page 42 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What project was the school's Invention Club working on?
explanation: The school's Invention Club was working on a phone-making initiative. This can be found on page 43 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What significant historical location did the students visit in Badagry?
explanation: The students at stardom school visited the First Storey Building in Nigeria, the Slave Market and the Point of No Return, the Seriki Abass Slave Museum as well as other historical sights in Badagry. This can be found on page 46 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What tourist attractions in Bauchi did the students explore?
explanation: The Yakari Games Reserve is located in Bauchi. It is one of the locations that the Stardom students have been to. This can be found on page 45 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What was the major challenge that Bepo faced during his NIN validation?
explanation: Bepo had a nasty experience at the NIN office because the network glitches were terrible. This can be found on page 53 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What performances during the send-off caused Bepo to become deeply emotional?
explanation: The Canoe dance is a dance of the Badagry people. The performance reminds him of his time at the Heritage Museum in Badagry and he his reminded of the brutal treatment of slaves by the white men. This makes him emotional. This can be found on page 55-56 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What year was the Ikogosi Warm Sprigs discovered?
explanation: This can be found on page 44 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What lesson does the novel teach about migration?
options: Migration should always be the last option
Migration brings mixed blessings and challenges
Migration is a betrayal of one's roots
explanation: The "migration tales" in the novel show that migration is a mixed bag of blessings and challenges. While some enjoy the benefits of the countries they relocated to, others like Hope face the bad side of migration.

subject: English Language
q: What was Bepo's relationship with Mrs Ibidun Gloss?
options: She deeply respected and appreciated his contributions
explanation:
Mrs. Ibidun Gloss was the Managing Director (MD) of Stardom Schools, which means she was Mr. Bepo’s direct supervisor. As the head of the school’s administration, she had the authority to oversee his work, make key decisions, and evaluate his performance.

While she respected and valued Mr. Bepo’s contributions, their relationship was first and foremost a professional one, where she had the final say on school matters. Therefore, the most accurate answer is that she was his supervisor rather than simply an admirer of his work.

subject: English Language
q: What caused Mr Bepo's emotional breakdown during the school assembly?
options: His upcoming relocation to the UK
explanation: Mr. Bepo’s breakdown was due to internal conflict and pressure to leave Stardom Schools for the UK. This can be found on page 10 of the novel where the reason for his breakdown is finally revealed.

subject: English Language
q: What is the boarding fee per session at Stardom Schools?
explanation: The boarding fee is N165,000 per session. This is a reduction from the original N250,000. This can be found on page 6 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why was the boarding fee at Stardom Schools reduced?
explanation: The boarding fee at Stardom school was reduced as a strategy to encourage punctuality among students. A lot of the students live very far away from school and so are not able to make it on time. The reduction of the boarding fees allowed a lot of parents to afford to enrol their students at the boarding houses. This can be found on page 6 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: The theme central to the novel is
options: The corruption in the Nigerian education system
The struggles of students in private schools
The decline of traditional Nigerian values
The challenges of migration and identity
explanation: The novel's central focus is the discussion of migration challenges and the crisis of identity. Bepo, as he prepares to relocate abroad, contemplates the different tales of those who migrated abroad. He also considered the impact this migration has on his identity. At the Heritage Museum in Badagry, he is reminded of the brutal history of slavery and feels that this voluntary migration to the lands of those who once colonised us is a form of "new slavery".

subject: English Language
q: How does The Lekki Headmaster portray the issue of "Japa"?
options: As a necessary step for career growth
As a personal and moral dilemma
As a privilege for the wealthy
As an easy decision for most Nigerians
explanation: The novel portrays the "Japa" issue as a personal and moral dilemma. The protagonist's journey throughout the novel is a personal journey. He contemplates every step he has made in both his personal and professional life to see how far he has come and to determine whether or not it would be a good idea to relocate abroad. Bepo understands that, on the one hand, travelling abroad has its benefits, such as increased pay, access to better healthcare and being closer to his family. But, he also understands that leaving Nigeria would mean abandoning his students whom he had nurtured. This is the personal and moral dilemma that most Nigerians face when they try to decide whether or not to relocate abroad.

subject: English Language
q: What is the central physical setting of the novel?
explanation: The central physical setting of the novel is Stardom Schools, Lagos. It is here that most of the events in the novel occur.

subject: English Language
q: Which setting in the novel serves as a point of psychological conflict for Bepo?
explanation: The setting that serves as a source of psychological conflict for Bepo is the Black Heritage Museum in Badagry. Here, he contemplates the brutal history of black slaves and begins to draw a parallel between this history and the contemporary voluntary migration of Nigerians to countries of those who once colonised us. This becomes a source of psychological conflict for Bepo.

subject: English Language
q: What narrative technique is commonly used in The Lekki Headmaster?
explanation: The most common narrative technique used in the novel is flashback. The writer uses this technique to relate events that happened in the past to the readers.

subject: English Language
q: What was Mr Bepo's long-term professional goal before considering migration?
options: Becoming the MD of Stardom Schools
Writing a book on educational reform
Retiring early and moving to his hometown
explanation:
This can be page 12 of the novel where it is revealed that Bepo, amongst other things, has thought of establishing his own school after having garnered a lot of experience in teaching and school management. His only fear was if he would have enough money to "float a standard school, given the taste he had already imbibed at Stardom." 

He had initially built a school - "Fruitful Future" but it had crumbled due to socio-economic challenges in the location.

subject: English Language
q: What does Chief Ogba demand after his son, Tosh, is publicly insulted?
options: A formal apology and publication in the school magazine
The immediate suspension of the student who insulted his son
Immediate expulsion of the guilty student
explanation: Chief Ogba, Tosh's father, demanded that Banky and his parents write separate letters of apology to both him and his son. He also insisted that the letters be published in the school magazine. This was after Banky had called Tosh "the son of an ex-convict" during his speech for the position of Social prefect. This can be found on page 43 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: How does the novel use irony to critique migration?
options: It shows how people regret leaving Nigeria.
It portrays Nigerian schools as superior to foreign institutions.
It contrasts the excitement of moving abroad with the struggles people face when they get there.
It portrays that migration is always a failure.
explanation: Irony is a powerful tool in the novel, used to critique societal norms and highlight the contradictions in modern Nigerian life. Bepo reflects on the irony of Africans willingly migrating to the countries of their former colonial masters, describing this as a “new slavery.”  The tales of Nigerians thriving or struggling abroad illustrate the irony of migration dreams. Stories like Jare’s regret over leaving a banking job to care for the elderly in London highlight the unpredictability of these pursuits.

subject: English Language
q: Why does the MD call an emergency meeting after discovering the cars at the school's land?
options: She believes they are using stolen school funds.
She want to commend them for financial growth.
She plans to reduce the teachers' salaries.
She is concerned about the teachers becoming financially independent
explanation: The emergency meeting of the board of Directors was called because the MD was concerned that the Cooperative allowed the teachers to be financially independent. She believes that the staff could rebel and jointly establish their own school with the money and steal the school's band ideal. This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Who makes up the board of Stardom Schools?
options: A mix of educators and business investors
explanation: The Board of Directors at Stardom Schools is made up of the MD's family members: the chairman, Chief Mrs Solape Bayo is the MD's mother, Martins Bayo is a non-executive director; and Oya Bayo, the last born, was the board's secretary. This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What did Bepo do before meeting with the director of Beesway the morning after he encountered the ritual practice?
options: He informed the police about the ritual activities.
He moved his belongings out of the school.
explanation: Bepo moved his belongings out of his room and the staff room the next morning before he went to see the director. This can be found on page 40 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Which poem came to Bepo's mind on his way to renew his passport?
explanation:
Bepo recalls the poem titled "Ibadan" by J.P. Clark as he journeyed through the capital city on his way to renew his passport. The poem gives an apt description of the Ibadan landscape that he sees as the Sienna he was in reached the top of the hilly bridge after Bere. 

Ibadan, grinning splash of rust

and gold-flung and scattered among 

seven hills like broken china in the sun. 

This can be found on page 51 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What was Mr. Fafore’s strategy to ensure that he came early to school even though he lives far away?
options: He slept in his car overnight
He rented an apartment near the school
He set multiple alarms on his phone
explanation: Although Mr Fafore lives quite far away, he always prepares his day so that he arrives very early. He wakes up as early as 4:00 am and sets out for school at 4:30 am. He would get to the school premises around 6:00 am, rest his head on his desk and snooze for about an hour before his colleagues arrived. This can be found on page 30 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Which of the following is NOT one of Bepo's ideas about teaching?
options: Teachers should maintain a strict authoritarian approach
Learning should be hands-on and engaging
explanation: Bepo believes that learning should focus on the growth and development of the students. He focuses on imparting the knowledge that the students need to be able to thrive when they get out into the world. He also believes that learning should be hands-on and engaging as he constantly encourages doing extra-curriculars that help students apply the knowledge they learnt in practical situations. This can be seen in the phone-making initiative that he had hoped to see to the end before he relocated to the UK. Finally, he believes that excursions can enhance the learning experience. He took the students out on excursions so that they can have a solid knowledge of the Nigerian environment before they travelled abroad to further their education. However, he does not believe in maintaining a strict authoritarian approach. He rather believes in resolving conflicts amicably.

subject: English Language
q: Why did Bepo choose to renew his passport in Ibadan?
options: It was more expensive to renew it in Lagos
He wanted to avoid the long queues in Lagos
He was transferred to the office in Ibadan
explanation: Bepo chose to renew his passport in Ibadan because there were longer queues in Lagos and paying to expedite the process could cost up to N200,000 to N250,000. He heard that he could travel to neighbouring states like Ogun, Oyo, Osun and Kwara where there would be less crowd; and so he settled for Ibadan. This can be found on page 48 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why did Stardom School stop the usual democratic election process for prefects?
options: Some students were engaging in negative campaigning
The school wanted teachers to appoint the prefects instead.
The Ministry of Education mandated a new selection process
Parents demanded to be involved in selecting the prefects
explanation: The usual democratic process of election of prefects was suspended at Stardom Schools because of the incident between Banky and Tosh. During Speech Day, Banky had said: "Instead of voting for the son of an ex-convict, cast your vote for me. You will never regret." He said this as part of his campaign and was directed at Tosh. This negative campaigning led to a chain of events including the suspension of this process of democratic election. This can be found on page 42 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why did Bepo spend the electricity tariff money he collected from his tenants?
options: He was out of a job and had no savings.
He needed to pay for his child's school fees
He used it for urgent medical expenses
explanation: This is one of the most embarrassing actions he has undertaken, according to the novel. When Bepo was out of a job and had no savings or no new employment in sight, he spent the N2,500 electricity tariff he collected on behalf of the tenants in his compound in Iyana Ipaja. This can be found on page 28 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: The following were hairstyles that Mrs Ignatius learnt in preparation for her migration abroad except
explanation: Mrs Ignatius learned different Yoruba braids like Suku, Onilegogoro and Kojusoko in preparation for her family's migration to Canada. She had learnt that learning this kind of skill would fetch a lot of money in Canada that could be used to support the family. This can be found on page 20 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What did Bepo's fellow tenant, Iya Matthew, pour on his head after he had embezzled the compound's electricity tariff?
explanation: Iya Matthew had grabbed a full bowl of elubo or cassava powder and dunked it on his head after the tenants discovered what Bepo had done. This can be found on page 28 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What was the topic of the debate at Bepo's send-off?
options: Migration: A curse or a blessing?
Who shapes the future: Teachers or parents?
The arts have contributed more to national development than the science
Public schools vs. private schools: Which is better?

subject: English Language
q: What eventually happened to Mr Ogo in the novel?
options: He relocated to the UK successfully
He opened a new private school
explanation:
Mr Ogo was the parent at Fruitful Future School who had recommended that Bepo engage in ritual practices that would help bring more students to the school. When Bepo rejects this offer, Mr Ogo withdraws his children from the school. However, ten years later, when Bepo is watching the evening news, he discovers that Mr Ogo has been arrested for the murder of a civil servant, a woman who had consulted him to help with her infertility. During the course of the treatment, Mr Ogo had collected a total of N9 million as a fee with no tangible results. 

When the woman confronts him about this and threatens to report him to the police, he invites her to his house and murders her. This can be found on page 39 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: According to the novel, the Gurara Waterfall was actually discovered in which year?
explanation: Contrary to the European claim that it was chanced upon by some Europeans in 1925, the Gurara Waterfall was discovered by a Gwari hunter in 1745. This can be found on page 45 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: According to the novel, who built the first-storey building in Nigeria?
explanation: Henry Townsend built the first-storey building in Nigeria in 1846. It is where Ajayi Crowther stayed and made the first translation of the Yoruba Bible. This can be found on page 46 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What does Bepo mean by the term "new slavery"?
options: The forced labour system in Nigerian schools
The economic challenges of the working class brought on by bad governance
The exploitation of teachers by private school owners
The voluntary migration to foreign countries to join their workforce
explanation: According to Bepo, "new slavery" is when "Africans themselves, especially Nigerians, in japa fit, voluntarily and desperately [walk] into the workforce of who seemed to be the yester masters". This means that we Africans, who are migrating, are voluntarily engaging in a new kind of slavery. This can be found on page 47 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What is the name of the passport office that Bepo plans to visit to renew his passport?
explanation: The name of the passport office in Ibadan is the Agodi-Gate Passport Office. This can be found on page 49 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What was inscribed on the banner at Bepo's send-off?
options: For He Gave Stardom His Very Best
We Will Miss You, Mr Bepo!
explanation: The banner had a large image of Bepo laughing gracefully and headlined FOR HE GAVE STARDOM HIS VERY BEST. It was subtitled "Memorable Farewell for the Most Committed Principal, Adewale Adebepo". This can be found on page 54 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What position did Bepo apply for when he first came to Stardom Schools?
explanation: Mr Bepo first applied for the position of the school's English Teacher. He was to teach SSS1-3. This was revealed during the MD's farewell speech and can be found on pages 56-57 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Who did Bepo sell his car to as he planned to relocate?
explanation: Mr Bepo had sold his Pathfinder Sports Utility Vehicle for N1.5 million to Stardom school's accountant, Mr Jeremi Amos. This can be found on page 60 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: How much did Bepo give his landlord's children as he left for the airport?
explanation: Bepo gave the landlord's children i.e. Jide and his sister, the sum of ₦5000 as a gift before he left for the airport.

subject: English Language
q: What course did Bepo study at the University of Benin?
explanation: Bepo studied English/ History Education at the University of Benin. This can be found on page 12 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What subject was Sola teaching at Stardom Schools before she relocated to the UK?
explanation: Sola was the Home Economics teacher at Stardom Schools before she relocated to the UK. This can be found on page 15 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Which historical figure, according to the novel, translated the Bible into Yoruba?
explanation: According to the novel, Bishop Ajayi Crowther was the one who translated the Bible into Yoruba. This can be found on page 46 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What literary device is used in the expression "under the world of tears"?
explanation: The phrase “under the world of tears” as used on page 8 of the novel metaphorically illustrates the depth of the principal’s emotional breakdown. It compares his sorrow to a physical state of being buried beneath tears.

subject: English Language
q: What was the mood at the beginning of the story when the principal walks to the podium?
explanation: The principal's uncharacteristic silence as he walked to the podium that morning, as well as his breakdown into tears, created a tense and bewildering atmosphere for the students. There was also a feeling of uncertainty as the students had never encountered such before. The principal they have come to know and depend on was behaving quite strangely. The heavy silence, the "perplexed eyes", and the shock from both students and staff all point to a tense and uncertain atmosphere. This can be inferred from the description on page 5 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: When Mr. Audu says, "the MD is a witch and wizard rolled into one!", what figure of speech is this?
explanation: Audu’s statement is metaphorical. He doesn’t mean it literally but uses it to exaggerate the MD’s cleverness and manipulative prowess in economics. This can be inferred from the descriptions on page 5 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: "You could see a rock carrying three other rocks on its head." What literary technique is being used?
explanation: This can be found on page 7 of the novel, where Ikenna was describing the "acrobatic" Jos landscape. He says: "You could see a rock carrying three other rocks on its head. You could find a small rock confidently backing another, big enough to give birth to it." The rocks are described as if they were performing a human action—carrying—which is a personification.

subject: English Language
q: What attitude do most teachers display towards Bepo's reluctance to relocate?
explanation: Many of Bepo's colleagues view his hesitation to relocate as irrational. They find it funny and think it stupid to pass on such a "golden opportunity". This can be inferred from the descriptions on pages 10 and 11.

subject: English Language
q: Why did the students expect comments from the principal after Ikenna's speech?
options: It was standard procedure at Stardom Schools
The principal always appreciated students after such good speeches
explanation:
Mr Bepo was always known for praising such efforts. This can be inferred from the description on pages 7-8 of the novel. 

"On a day like this, he would be on top of the world. He would regard Ikenna's outing as evidence of a good education Stardom offered, and which he believed every Nigerian child deserved. The students also expected him to highlight some terms Ikenna might not have articulated so well..." (page 7-8).

subject: English Language
q: Why is Jos described as "acrobatic" in Ikenna's speech?
options: because of the Jos native dancers
beacause of its terrain and rock formations
because the people perform circus tricks
explanation:
Rocks in Jos appear stacked in dramatic postures.

"You could see a rock carrying three other rocks on its head. You could find a small rock confidently backing another, big enough to give birth to it". (Page 7)

subject: English Language
q: What figure of speech is used in “...the microphone dropped on the floor, sending a vexatious clatter out of the twin sound boxes”?
explanation: “Clatter” mimics the sound made when the microphone hits the floor. This line can be found on page 6 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: The overall tone of the story can be described as
explanation:
The story strikes a balance between a reflective sadness, stemming from Bepo's emotional turmoil, and warm character insights. Bepo grapples with the decision of whether to leave his students for a trip to the UK. His visit to the Badagry Heritage Slave Museum becomes a pivotal moment as it prompts him to reflect on the idea of "new slavery." This reflection leads him to the realisation that he is not ready to become one of these modern slaves; instead, he chooses to remain at Stardom School to nurture his students.

Throughout the narrative, a melancholic atmosphere persists. It begins with Bepo's emotional breakdown during the assembly and continues as he contemplates various migration stories, particularly those of individuals who were unsuccessful in their migration attempts. Insights from these different people encourage him to be more reflective and to think critically about this important relocation decision.

subject: English Language
q: Why did Mr. Bepo finally decide to leave Nigeria?
options: To reunite with his family and avoid marital collapse
explanation: Bepo succumbed to family pressure; his wife and daughters had already moved to the UK, and his hesitation was straining their relationship. This can be found on page 10 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What was unique about Bepo’s initial job interview at Stardom?
options: It was done over the phone
He was interviewed by the founder
He had to bribe the MD's father
explanation: The founder, Chief Aje, hired Bepo personally after a one-on-one discussion, unlike many others who faced the school's interview panels. This can be found on page 56 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What is the name of the director of Beesway Group of School?
explanation: Mr Egi Meko is the founder of Beesway Group of School, the school that Mr Bepo worked in before he came to Stardom. He was the one who laughed off Bepo's correction of the phrase "Group of School" and refused to change the school's name. This can be found on page 34 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What shocking behaviour did a UK student allegedly exhibit toward a Nigerian teacher, according to the novel?
options: Insulting him with 'Are you an idiot?'
explanation: Bepo recalled the story as an example of culture shock for Nigerian teachers abroad. This can be found on page 13 of the novel where Bepo notes that he recalls reading the story of a Nigerian teacher who got settled the first day he walked into a class in a UK school and one of the students asked: "Are you an idiot?". Bepo, however, believes this is an isolated incident, if at all it is true, and should not deter him from relocating abroad to reunite with his family.

subject: English Language
q: What gesture impressed Sola when her daughter Betty was hospitalised in the UK?
options: Her husband was offered a job
Emergency care arrived in under 5 minutes
They were moved to a private hospital
explanation: The swift emergency response and compassionate care, including birthday gifts from doctors, touched her deeply. This can be found on page 17 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What kind of job did Akindele, the older Nigerian migrant, do when he first arrived in the US?
explanation: He worked first in freight loading, then in a correctional centre. This is one of the cautionary tales of migration that Bepo heard. It shows the constant grind in America by these immigrants. This can be found on page 18 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: What figure of speech is used in the expression "...wouldn't have to clean 'dishes', as folks uncharitably say about washing corpses."
explanation: The phrase "clean dishes," when used figuratively in the context of washing corpses, could be interpreted as a euphemism. Euphemisms are a type of figurative language where a mild or indirect term is used to replace something that might be considered harsh, blunt or unpleasant. In this case, "clean dishes" softens or disguises the grim reality of washing corpses, making it sound more mundane or routine.

subject: English Language
q: What city did Sola and her husband settle in within the UK?
explanation: Sola and her husband settled in Manchester due to lower rent and cost of living. They could not afford the monthly rent of up to 1,500 or 1,000 pounds in London, so they had to stay in Manchester, where they rented a two-bedroom apartment for 650 pounds monthly.

subject: English Language
q: What career did Bepo's wife Seri practice abroad?
explanation: Seri, his wife, was already working as a nurse in the UK. This can be found on page 11 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: “Tears trickled out of his eyes… More tears streamed from both eyes, competitively…” is an example of—
explanation: These words can be found on page 5 of the novel where they were used to describe Bepo's emotional state at the beginning of the text. Tears are personified as if they are racing or competing with each other.

subject: English Language
q: “He brought his palms together like a supplicant” is an example of—
explanation: This is used to describe how Mr Bepo tried to shield his teary face from the view of the students and staff. It compares his posture to that of someone begging or praying using “like”. This is the use of simile. The words can be found on page 5 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: The Yoruba proverb "The Oyingbo market never finds out a certain person did not even turn up" means
explanation: The Yoruba proverb Oja Oyingbo ko mo' p'enikan o wa' ("The Oyingbo market never finds out a certain person did not even turn up") means that the market is "ever bustling" or always busy. The proverb can be found on page 60 of the novel. When Mr Bepo was said to have gone there to purchase some food items that his wife instructed him to bring along with him.

subject: English Language
q: "If you beat my height, you can’t beat my eyes.” What was Bepo trying to communicate here?
options: Physical strength surpasses all other traits
No act of cheating will go uncaught
Height is the least important quality of a person
A competitive spirit always guarantee victory
explanation: Bepo says this whenever he is invigilating an examination. Whenever he is present, according to him, no act of cheating will go uncaught. This can be found on page 19 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q: Why does the narrator employ a conversational tone in the narration of Bepo's story?
options: To show multiple perspectives on the subject matter
explanation: The friendly, conversational tone of the narrator in The Lekki Headmaster mirrors everyday Nigerian speech. This makes the story feel authentic, relatable and accessible to the reader. The reader can thus understand that the tale is a tale of everyday Nigerian life and that the subject matter applies to all of us.

subject: English Language
q: When the director of Beesway references the Yoruba proverb about vegetables plucked from a dumpsite, what was he trying to communicate to Bepo?
options: People who feel guilty defend what wasn't questioned
People on the bottom can be destined to do greater things
explanation: Mr Meko references the story of a hawker who plucked vegetables from a filthy dumpsite, and when a buyer calls to her, having no intention to probe the source of the product, immediately begins to assure the value of feigned quality. The hawker then retorts: "These vegetables are not from any dumpsite". With this proverb, Mr Meko is trying to hint at how people who feel guilty often defend what wasn't questioned.

subject: English Language
q: What figure of speech is used in "He would look at the ceiling and at the faces before him, as though he had just returned from a dreamy wonderland”?
explanation: This expression can be found on page 6 of the novel, where Bepo is in a state of emotional turmoil and has been taken to the VP's office. While they are trying to inquire what the reason for his emotional outburst is, he stared at the ceiling instead. His detachment from reality is compared to the detachment one gets when they have just returned from a dreamy wonderland.

subject: English Language
q: “Mr. Audu, the Fine Arts teacher, who was a bunch of biting humour…” What does this description of Mr Audu mean?
options: He is sarcastic and has sharp, critical wit
He is known for his apt innuendos
explanation: This expression can be found on page 6 of the novel where Mr Audu was described to be "a bunch of biting humour". He complained about how the MD was "a witch and wizard rolled into one" because of the way she was able to find a way to offset the cost from the Boarding House discount by increasing the Excursion fees. This is one of the examples that illustrate how Mr Audu is sarcastic and full of sharp, critical wit.

subject: English Language
q: “...his foot-dragging over the matter had pushed his marriage to the brink.” What does "foot-dragging" mean as used in the novel?
explanation: "Foot-dragging" means delay or unwillingness to take timely action on an issue. This expression can be found on page 10 of the novel. Where the reason for Bepo's emotional outburst is eventually revealed. The character's procrastination or hesitation had a detrimental effect on his marriage and he has no other option than to succumb to their demands of relocating to the UK.

subject: English Language
q: Mrs. Ibidun Gloss expressed deep appreciation to Bepo for contributing what she called an "unrivalled quota" to the growth of Stardom. What does "unrivalled quota" as used in the novel mean?
options: Bepo's incomparable and exceptional contribution to the growth of the school
His savings over the years which is not comparable to the contributions of others
His impactful contribution to the development of the school's building
His buying of a large chunk of the school's shares
explanation: This expression can be found on page 56 of the novel where the MD was saying her farewell speech to Bepo, who was leaving stardom. She was trying to express her appreciation for the overwhelming and incomparable contribution that he has made to the growth of the school. The phrase "unrivalled quota" highlights that Bepo’s efforts were unmatched and outstanding, setting them apart from others. The word "unrivalled" emphasizes the uniqueness and superiority of Bepo's contribution, while "quota" refers to the portion or effort provided by Bepo to Stardom's growth.

subject: English Language
q: Which of the following is not a figure of speech used in J.P. Clark's poem "Ibadan" as referenced in the novel?
explanation:
In J.P. Clark's "Ibadan":

Ibadan, running splash of rust

and gold-flung and scattered among

seven hills like broken china in the sun. 

The poem makes use of metaphor in the first line where the rustiness of the roofs is compared to a "running splash". Simile is used with the comparison of the rusted roofs to broken china in the sun. Imagery is also used because we can picture the descriptions given in our minds. However, onomatopoeia, the use of words that imitate or resemble the sound they describe, is not used in the poem.

subject: English Language
q: The following are current socio-economic realities depicted in the novel except
options: The declining condition of Nigerian universities
explanation:
The novel addresses several socio-economic realities, including the Japa trend, which describes the mass emigration of Nigerians seeking better opportunities abroad. It highlights the challenges faced by bureaucracies, particularly when obtaining essential travel documents. Additionally, it portrays the struggles of middle-class professionals, such as teachers who are often undervalued and underpaid, as well as doctors and other health workers who choose to leave the country for "greener pastures" due to a lack of opportunities at home.

However, while the declining state of Nigerian universities is a significant socio-economic issue, it is not one of the themes explored in this novel.

subject: English Language
q: One of the first things a child notices in birds is
explanation: The passage explicitly states, "One of the first things that a child notices is how noisy many kinds of birds are".

subject: English Language
q: What makes it easy to identify a species of birds in the wood?
explanation: The passage explains that "many birds make characteristic calls, and with practice you can tell which species are in a wood just from these calls without seeing the birds at all".

subject: English Language
q: Flocks of finches could easily become separated
options: when they get injured by other birds
when they get hungry after being separated from other birds
when they see predators hovering in the sky
when they hunt for insects or seeds on the branches of trees
explanation: The passage states, "When flocks of finches are looking for insects or seeds on the branches of trees, they could easily become separated...".

subject: English Language
q: The small birds have
explanation:
The passage states that “at the sight of a bird of prey, a small bird will give its flying predator call as an alarm to other birds in the neighbourhood.”
This suggests that small birds use a particular type of call: "the flying predator call" to warn others of danger.

subject: English Language
q: When a bird of prey is sighted, a small bird
explanation: The text states that "at the sight of a bird of prey, a small bird will give its flying predator call as an alarm to other birds in the neighbourhood".

subject: English Language
q: Fill the gap labelled 6
explanation: The "United Nations Security Council" (UNSC) is the official and proper name of this specific UN organ.

subject: English Language
q: Fill the gap labelled 7
explanation: The standard phrase used in the UN Charter is "the maintenance of international peace and security."

subject: English Language
q: Fill the gap labelled 8
explanation: "Peace and security" is the fixed legal phrase for the Council's mandate.

subject: English Language
q: Fill the gap labelled 9
explanation: In this context, "members" refers to the individual seats/countries. "Members" (plural) is grammatically correct following the number fifteen.

subject: English Language
q: Fill the gap labelled 10
explanation: In international bodies, member states exercise their power by casting a vote on resolutions.

subject: English Language
q: Fill in the gap labelled 11
explanation: The founding document of the United Nations is called the UN Charter.

subject: English Language
q: Fill in the gap labelled 12
explanation: Under Article 39 of the Charter, the Council determines the existence of any "threat to the peace."

subject: English Language
q: Fill in the gap labelled 13
explanation: In legal and diplomatic terms, the groups involved in a conflict or dispute are referred to as the parties.

subject: English Language
q: Fill the gap labelled 14
explanation: A "settlement" is the formal term for the resolution or ending of a dispute or conflict.

subject: English Language
q: Fill the gap labelled 15
explanation: When diplomacy fails, the UN can impose economic or diplomatic sanctions to enforce its decisions without using military force.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Alabi Garba's The Lekki Headmaster

The school nurse, Mrs Titi, fetched a handkerchief and offered it to him because
options: she always went about with an extra handkerchief
the Physics teacher, Pastor Ope Wande, had prayed over it
it was all she could do at the moment
she didn't have any drug immediately available
explanation:
This can be found on page 5 of the novel. The principal, Mr. Bepo Adewale, was weeping uncontrollably during the morning assembly. While several staff members, including the Vice Principal, gathered around to find out what was wrong, he continued to sob freely and could not utter a word.

Mrs. Titi (Nurse Titi) offered the handkerchief to him because he was a "weeper" in that moment of distress, and along with other staff, she began helping him back to his office as they were unable to get him to stop crying or explain his plight.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Alabi Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

"Red-tapism is found everywhere" means
options: Red tape worm is found everywhere
explanation:
In the novel, this concept is illustrated through Mr. Bepo’s experience while trying to renew his international passport:

The term refers to excessive bureaucracy or adherence to official rules and formalities that prevent or delay action.

Bepo faced numerous hurdles, including tales of new rules, hassles for applicants, and the non-availability of booklets. Although the government claimed the process could be done online without bribes, Bepo found that the online system was deliberately slow or erratic to frustrate applicants. Because of these excessive formalities, Bepo felt forced to use an "agent" to bypass the crowd and expedite the capturing process.

This whole ordeal can be found in chapter 10 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster

'If you beat my height, you can't beat my eyes' is a statement by
explanation: Bepo told students jovially that he was created to be a principal. He believed that because of his six-footer height and prominent eyeballs, no act of cheating could go uncaught. He would declare, "If you beat my height, you can't beat my eyes," whenever he was supervising an exam. This can be found on page 19 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

How did Sola and her husband travel to London from Manchester every day?
explanation: Sola explained to Bepo that they lived in Manchester because they could not afford the high rents in London.She mentioned that travel by rail from Manchester to London was about 30 minutes. This can be found on page 16 of the novel

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster

"Yes....That is what you paid the extra amount for. If you had followed the normal channel, you would still be there with them."

To whom was this said?
explanation: This was said to Bepo by a passport officer in Ibadan after he paid an extra amount to expedite his passport renewal. The officer confirmed that the extra payment allowed Bepo to bypass the large crowd following the "normal channel." This can be found on page 52 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

"So, you have taken your madness to this level?"

Where was the statement made?
explanation: This statement was made by the director of Beesway Group of School, Mr. Egi Meko, to Bepo. Meko accused Bepo of plotting to destroy the school by discussing a grammatical error in the school's name with a parent during a PTA meeting. This can be found on page 35 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Bepo considered establishing a school because
explanation: Bepo would not mind establishing his own school because he had "garnered a lot of experience about teaching and school management" and it would give him an "opportunity to push his passion for moulding the young". This can be found on page 12 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Bibi's scream diverted Mrs Ladele's attention from a movie on
explanation: Mrs. Ladele was watching a movie where she was captivated by an "imminent bubble burst." Unknown to the doctor in the film, the doctor's husband was responsible for the pregnancy of the doctor's best friend. Mrs. Ladele was watching this painful truth unfold when she heard Bibi's scream. This can be found on page 22 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Bepo's retirement plan suggests that he was
explanation: Bepo began attending entrepreneurship seminars and planning for life after age 55, showing he was "thinking seriously" and had "pictured himself" in a position to employ others and pay taxes. This can be found on page 11 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

What did Bepo believe was crucial for success in the transportation business?
explanation: Bepo believed that a major challenge in transportation was that many commercial drivers lacked enlightened business practices and were not trustworthy, which ruined the business dream. This can be found on page 12 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster. 

What was the essence of open days at Stardom Schools?
options: to update parents on academic activities
to give teachers an opportunity to relax
explanation: For the management of Stardom Schools, Open Days served as an important opportunity to update parents on academic activities. It was a day intended for staff to have personal encounters with parents, which the school encouraged and sometimes compelled. While it was a time for parents to give gifts to specific staff , it was also a day when teachers expected to receive various complaints from parents. This can be found on page 27 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

One of the challenges faced in General Hospitals cited in the novel is
explanation: The text notes that many people had reservations about government (general) hospitals. They dread them because of the large crowds they attracted. These hospitals were also described as being bogged down by inefficiency and corruption, often leaving doctors overwhelmed. This can be found on page 22 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster

The contestant for the position of a prefect must have spent ... in the school.
explanation: To be an aspirant for a prefect position at Stardom Schools, the student must have been in the school for up to two sessions. Additionally, the contestant was expected to have a good moral history, be above average academically and must not owe any school fees. This can be found on page 41 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Bibi is always scared of Mr Ayesoro because of his
explanation: Bibi was scared of Mr. Ayesoro because of the deep tribal marks he flaunted on his face. Because of these prominent incisions, students nicknamed him "Mr. Owala," a derogatory term for a person with wild facial marks. This can be found on page 22 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

The stories of Nigerian migrants such as Sola, Hope, Riike and Akindele showed that, for thos who have 'ja pa'
options: it is the final goodbye to Nigeria
those still in Nigeria are wasting their time
life is not a bed of roses
there is no cause for alarm
explanation: The collective stories of these migrants demonstrated that moving abroad (the "ja pa" phenomenon) involves significant struggles. Hope was abandoned by his wife and lost his sponsorship. Akindele described the exhaustion of working "for every dime" and every "cent," noting there is no easy job in America. Sola lived in cramped, non-furnished housing and changed jobs frequently just to survive. These tales can be found in chapter 3 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Why was the Government teacher named 'Owala'?
explanation: "Owala" is a derogatory Yoruba appellation for a person with wild facial marks. Mr. Ayesoro was popular for the deep tribal marks he flaunted on his face. This can be found on page 22 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster

How did Stardom Schools curb late-coming?
options: By publishing names of latecomers in the school magazine
explanation: Stardom Schools managed to greatly reduce lateness by lowering its boarding fees from N250,000 to N165,000 per session. This incentive led over 80 percent of parents to move their children to the boarding house, resulting in almost all students arriving by 7:45am for assembly. This can be found on page 6 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Bepo worked in Stardom for over
explanation: Mr. Bepo had been an inspiring pillar at Stardom for 24 years (which is over two decades). This can be found on page 56 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
The question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

What joke did Bepo tell his students concerning his big eyes?
options: his big eyes will always catch whoever was cheating
he said the eyes were always open even when he slept
Bepo said he was also an eye-master
he said his mum ate an owl when she carried his pregnancy
explanation: Bepo jovially told his students that his big eyes will always catch whoever was cheating. He claimed he was "created to be a principal" and would tell students, "If you beat my height, you can't beat my eyes" during exams. This can be found on page 19 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

"It's like hanging a snake in the roof and going to bed". Who made this statement in The Lekki Headmaster?
explanation: The statement was made by Chief Mrs. Solape Bayo, the chairman of the board of directors (and the MD's mother). She was expressing fear that the staff might use the large sum of money in their cooperative society to "rebel" and establish their own rival school. This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

No sooner had Tobi left the house _____ it began to rain.
explanation:
In English grammar, certain phrases are known as correlative conjunctions. They are pairs of words that always go together.

  • No sooner... than: This is the standard pair. Because "sooner" is a comparative adjective (ending in -er), it must be followed by "than," just like "faster than" or "taller than."

  • Hardly/Scarcely... when: This is a different structure often confused with the one above. If the sentence had started with "Hardly had Tobi left...," then "when" would be the correct choice.

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

What goes around _____ around.
explanation: This is a common English proverb, and its wording is fixed. Proverbs usually follow a specific structure that doesn't change based on conversational context. The first part of the sentence uses the simple present tense (goes). The second part should also use the simple present tense (comes).

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

The boy _____ the flute for about thirty minutes.
explanation: This tense is used for an action that started in the past and is still continuing into the present. The phrase "for about thirty minutes" indicates this. The structure for this tense is has/have + been + verb-ing.

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

Each of the answers ______ correct.
explanation: The word "Each" is the actual subject of the sentence, not "answers". In English, "Each" is always treated as a singular pronoun. Even though "answers" is plural, the verb must agree with "Each."

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

Each of the reports ______ been carefully reviewed by the committee
explanation:
The subject is "Each". Even though "reports" is plural, the verb must agree with the singular "Each".

The sentence structure requires an auxiliary verb to pair with "been" to form the present perfect tense. Has is the singular form

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

The ____ of armed robbery has been on the increase.
explanation: Incidence refers to the rate or frequency of something happening (e.g., crime rates). Incident refers to a single, specific event. Because the sentence mentions being "on the increase," it is talking about the frequency/rate of the crime, so "incidence" is the correct choice.

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

The doctor ____ him to take his medicine regularly.
explanation: Advise is the verb. It is the action of giving a recommendation. Advice, the noun, is the recommendation itself.  Since the doctor is performing an action in the past, the correct past-tense verb is "advised."

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

He was praised for the ______ achievement.
explanation: "Laudable" is an adjective meaning "deserving praise and commendation".

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

It is high time you _____ visiting that friend of yours.
explanation:
When you use the phrases "It is time," "It is high time" or "It is about time" followed by a subject (in this case, "you"), you must use the Past Simple tense of the verb.

Even though the sentence refers to the present or future, we use the past tense to indicate that the action is overdue or should have happened already. This is known as the unreal past or the subjunctive mood.

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

Put Chioma's meat in _____ pack.
explanation:
The standard hierarchy for adjectives in English is often remembered by the acronym OSASCOMP:

Order Category Examples from the Question
1 Opinion Shiny
2 Size (Not present)
3 Age New
4 Shape (Not present)
5 Color Red
6 Origin (Not present)
7 Material (Not present)
8 Purpose Lunch (describing the type of pack)

Putting them together gives you: "shiny new red lunch pack."

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

As trained teachers, Joke teaches _______ better than Jumoke.
explanation: The word "better" is a comparative adjective. When we want to emphasise or strengthen a comparative, we use specific intensifiers. "Far" is a standard intensifier used with comparative forms (e.g., far better, far taller, far more expensive). "Much" is another common choice (much better).

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

The teacher asked the students to submit their assignments before the end of ____ week
explanation: In this sentence, the context implies a deadline for a future action. "The teacher asked the students to submit..." indicates an instruction for something that needs to happen after the request was made. "Before the end of next week" is a standard way to set a future deadline.

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

If I had worked hard, I _______ very high marks in the exam.
explanation: This is a Third Conditional sentence, used to talk about a past situation that didn't happen and its imaginary result. The structure is: If + Past Perfect (had worked), ... would have + Past Participle (scored).

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

It had been raining before the party started, _____
explanation:
Question tags follow a specific rule: they must use the same auxiliary verb used in the main sentence, but in the opposite form (positive sentence = negative tag).

  • Main Verb: "had been" (Positive)

  • Tag: "hadn't" (Negative) + "it" (Subject)

subject: English Language
q:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

That Fulani man has a large _____ of sheep.
explanation:
In English, specific groups of animals have unique collective nouns.

  • Flock: The correct term for a group of sheep or birds.

  • Herd: used for cattle, elephants, or goats.

  • Swine: An old-fashioned term for pigs, not a collective noun.
